name: US and OECD Short-Term Interest Rates

book_quotes.quote: Figure 16.7 shows the US interest rate to alongside a weighted average of major OECD countries from 1960 to 2011, demonstrating that from the 1980s onward policy-induced decreases in interest rates were characteristic of the whole capitalist center. With short-term interest rates hovering around zero there is no further room to maneuver on this front.

The US interest rate has been described previously. ioecd = US trading partner interest rates. US trading partner weights taken from the Federal Reserve Board Indexes of the Foreign Exchange Value of the Dollar (http://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/h10/Weights/) were used to derive a weighted average of interest rates taken from the International Financial Statistics (IFS) of the International Monetary Fund (IMF). I am greatly indebted to Amr Ragab for these calculations.

Figure 16.6 US Rate of Interest, 1947-2011 (3-Month T-Bill). The interest rate is the 3-month T-bill rate, available in table 73, first data column in The Economic Report of the President published by the BEA, http://www.gpoaccess.gov/eop/tables10.html.

primary_source.agency: IMF (OECD weighted average) and US Treasury / Federal Reserve (US rate)
primary_source.publication: IMF International Financial Statistics (IFS) for trading-partner rates; Economic Report of the President table 73 for US 3-month T-bill
primary_source.table_or_series_id: IMF IFS 'Money Market Rate' / 'Treasury Bill Rate' (multiple country codes); ERP Table 73 col 1 = 3-month T-bill
primary_source.units: Percent per annum (decimal in some subseries)
primary_source.license: IMF data: subscription / public access for headline series; US federal data: public domain
primary_source.available_through: IMF IFS database; FRED (US); OECD.Stat MEI Short-Term Interest Rates

formula: i_OECD = sum_c(w_c * i_c), where w_c = Fed H.10 trade weights (broad index) and i_c = country c short-term rate from IMF IFS
components: US 3-month Treasury Bill rate (FRED TB3MS / Economic Report of President Table 73)
Federal Reserve H.10 trading partner weights (https://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/h10/Weights/)
IMF IFS country-level short-term interest rates (Money Market Rate / Treasury Bill Rate)

concerns: Direct continuation; no methodology change since 1934
OECD aggregate uses different weighting than Fed H.10 broad index (GDP-PPP vs trade weights); methodology mismatch with Shaikh's bespoke construction.
Per CD2 S098, replication requires Fed H.10 weights + IMF IFS country series, not OECD pre-aggregated

extension_candidates.subseries_label: Euro Area short-term rate (for EU fill-in subseries S094D)
concerns: Pre-1999 euro area requires synthetic ECU rate

methodology_notes: Per CD2 S098, the composite Shaikh series has five subseries (S094-A through S094-E in CD2 = S098-A through S098-E): A = OECD-wide annual rate; B = US rate from Appendix 6.8 profit rate file; C = OECD MEI alt; D = ratio OECD/EU; E = combined OECD% with EU fill-in.

The OECD weighted average is a Shaikh-original construction credited to Amr Ragab — direct re-replication needs IMF IFS country rates + Fed H.10 weights. Modern replacements: FRED IR3TBB01...M156N family or OECD MEI IRSTCI01... give similar but not identical series.
The US 3-month T-bill in Fig 16.6 (covered as background context for Fig 16.7) is taken from ERP Table 73 col 1, equivalent to FRED TB3MS annual.
The ERP URL (gpoaccess.gov/eop/tables10.html) has been replaced by https://www.govinfo.gov/app/collection/erp; legacy URL is no longer maintained.
open_questions: Replicating Ragab's weighted OECD average requires reconstructing Fed H.10 trade weights for each year — these weights have themselves been revised; choose latest revision or vintage-as-of weights?
Should extension switch to OECD MEI pre-aggregated short-term rate for simplicity, sacrificing methodological fidelity? Phase 4 adequacy decision.
Original ERP URL is dead — extension must use FRED TB3MS or current govinfo.gov ERP.

review_history.notes: Ported from CD2 S098 with verbatim quotes from p. 733 (Fig 16.7 narrative) and Appendix 16.1 p. 899.
